metadata.title: Integrating Big Data Into Evaluation: R Code for Topic Identification and Modeling

abstract: Despite the rising popularity of big data, there is speculation that evaluators have been slow adopters of these new statistical approaches. Several possible reasons have been offered for why this is the case: ethical concerns, institutional capacity, and evaluator capacity and values. In this method note, we address one of these barriers and aim to build evaluator capacity to integrate big data analytics into their studies. We focus our efforts on a specific topic modeling technique referred to as latent Dirichlet allocation (LDA) because of the ubiquitousness of qualitative textual data in evaluation. Given current equity debates, both within evaluation and the communities in which we practice, we analyze 1,796 tweets that use the hashtag #equity with the R packages topicmodels and ldatuning to illustrate the use of LDA. Furthermore, a freely available workbook for implementing LDA topic modeling is provided as Supplemental Material Online.

sections.paper_id: Cintron and Montrosse-Moorhead - 2022 - Integrating Big Data Into Evaluation R Code for Topic Identification and Modeling
paragraphs: Big data use is becoming endemic to social science research. This "data deluge," catalyzed by technological trends including the Internet of Things, cloud computing, and smart devices, is producing large volumes of rich, heterogeneous data (e.g., education, health, social network, financial, marketing) collected at a velocity (e.g., real time, moment to moment) that is unprecedented (Finucane et al., 2018;Oussous et al., 2018). In 2010, the Office of Management and Budget and the Office of Science and Technology Policy declared that big data is a national challenge and priority analogous to health care and national security (Kaisler et al., 2013). In 2012, the National Science Foundation, the National Institutes of Health, the U.S. Geological Survey, the Departments of Defense and Energy, and the Defense Advanced Research Projects Agency advanced a US$200 million joint research and development initiative to build the U.S. nation's " . . . understanding of the technologies needed to manipulate and mine massive amounts of information; apply that knowledge to other scientific fields; address national goals in health, energy, defense, and education; and train more researchers to work with those technologies" (Mervis, 2012, p. 22). That same year, the United Nations released a white paper arguing for the relevance of big data to evaluation (Letouzé, 2012).

The evaluation community has been slower than other decision-making sciences to integrate big data into their repertoire of methods (Bamberger, 2017;Letouzé et al., 2016). Several reasons have been suggested. Some of these reasons are not unique to big data but rather are about the use and analysis of any type of data. Ethical concerns are an example. Regardless of data type, evaluators must ensure that relevant ethical principles, like the American Evaluation Association Guiding Principles for Evaluators (2018), are upheld. Moreover, any time a new method, approach, analysis technique, and the like is developed, institutional capacity is an important factor to consider. For example, what governance structures exist which provide access to data and protect that data? What institutional linkages are in place to connect evaluators with data scientists, statisticians, and auditors? Other reasons offered are unique to big data and include differences in values and differences in evaluator capacity. For example, the data science and big data community have different values than the evaluation community when it comes to issues of bias and data quality, the role of theory, and the merits of predictive analytics combined with machine learning (Anderson, 2008;Bamberger, 2017). Even when these value differences can be negotiated, an impediment to use in evaluation is that most evaluators lack big data knowledge and skills.

Helping evaluators develop the ability to use big data techniques is important for two reasons. One, like other methodological revolutions before (e.g., advances in quasi-experimental designs for causal inference, the development of hierarchical linear modeling for dealing with nested data), we see value in helping evaluators build their capacity to use big data. Two, we believe that studies that couple big data with evaluative thinking can generate real life, practice-based examples of how to address concerns related to ethics, institutional capacity, and negotiating big data and evaluation values.

In this method note, we provide a case example and discussion of a big data approach known as topic modeling. Topic modeling is an approach that aims to derive themes (called "latent topics" or "semantic structures" in the big data literature) that characterize vast quantities of text data in a rapid timeframe (Figgou & Pavlopoulos, 2015). Multiple techniques for implementing topic modeling exist (e.g., latent semantic analysis and singular value decomposition). Here, however, we focus on latent Dirichlet allocation (LDA) because of its popularity in the big data community and its ability to handle multiple meanings of a word (i.e., polysemy). We also focus on LDA topic modeling because we believe it holds particular relevance for evaluation.

The relevance of LDA topic modeling for evaluation is more easily seen in potential applications. Consider the situation where an evaluation team wants to engage stakeholders but is limited by their ability to collect information from a large number of stakeholders due to budget constraints. LDA topic modeling would allow for evaluators to collect data from a large group of stakeholders through an open-ended question (e.g., "what should be the focus of this evaluation?") and analyze the resulting data to identify the major ideas that are important overall and to different stakeholder groups (e.g., parents, community members). Or consider the situation where the evaluation team needs to summarize a large number of extant program or policy reports. LDA topic modeling is a viable option in this situation for mapping the literature by identifying the major themes embedded. Or consider the situation where a team is tasked with evaluating an intervention (e.g., professional development program) that has moved entirely online due to the COVID-19 pandemic, and as a result, significant volumes of text data (e.g., hundreds or thousands of discussion board postings) have been generated. LDA topic modeling could be used to identify dominant themes in these postings, and these themes could then serve as a springboard for the team to do a more traditional 413 Cintron and Montrosse-Moorhead thematic or content analysis on a subset of data to further investigate a theme of interest. In this way, LDA topic modeling would be one component of a mixed-method process. These examples, among others discussed elsewhere in this method note, provide a snapshot of the potential relevance and utility of LDA topic modeling for the evaluation community.

This method note aims to provide guidance to evaluators on how to implement topic modeling using open-source software and, ideally, motivate the use of topic modeling in evaluation. Before illustrating a case example of LDA topic modeling, we begin by providing background on big data, topic modeling, LDA, and their possible role in evaluation. We then describe the implementation of LDA topic modeling in R with a pedagogical case example that uses a collection of 1,796 openended tweets with the #equity hashtag. Finally, we end with an additional discussion of the potential uses of LDA topic modeling in evaluation. Aligned with our focus to help build evaluator capacity, a free workbook for implementing LDA topic modeling in R is provided as Supplemental Material Online (as both an HTML and R markdown file). 1 This Supplemental Material also includes steps to construct an equity tweets data set for use with the workbook. 2

sections.paper_id: Cintron and Montrosse-Moorhead - 2022 - Integrating Big Data Into Evaluation R Code for Topic Identification and Modeling
paragraphs: For this method note, we define big data as "an 'integrated approach to research and development' (including development evaluation) that involves three interlinked components": data generation, data analytics, and data ecosystems (UN Global Pulse, 2016, p. 34). 3 Data generation involves the production and collection of large volumes of data. The deluge of big data produces data in various formats and content, regularly characterized as the variety of big data (e.g., see the five Vs of big data 4 in Katal et al., 2013). For example, big data is often characterized as coming in the form of either structured (e.g., a wide file with IDs, demographic variables, and variables of interest), semistructured (e.g., an XML log file of examinee test-taking behavior), or unstructured data (e.g., a file with a mix of images and text). The content of big data could include data from web pages, emails, log files, social media (e.g., tweets or Facebook likes), documents (e.g., scientific journal articles), or sensor devices (e.g., an accelerometer).

In the context of evaluation, data analytics, whether with big data or not, requires that data science, data analysis, data visualization, and evaluation methods and thinking be combined to answer evaluation questions and that our conclusions be presented in a user-friendly format. Of particular importance for this method note is the emergence of machine learning, a technical field at the intersection of computer science and statistics that is central to the core practices of artificial intelligence and data science researchers (Jordan & Mitchell, 2015). Specifically, machine learning focuses on questions about how we can build software that learns and improves through experiences with data. This focus has led to the " . . . adoption of data-intensive machine-learning methods . . . throughout science, technology, and commerce, leading to more evidence-based decision-making across many walks of life . . . ." (Jordan & Mitchell, 2015, p. 255).

Data ecosystems refer to the ecosystems that link data, organizations, individuals, data producers, analysts, and users of data. Within evaluation, "the concept of community is important because the interpretation of big data requires blending of techniques from different cultures including business, the social sciences, and the computer sciences" (UN Global Pulse, 2016, p. 37). Shifting the focus from the types of data produced to the broader data ecosystem is one way in which evaluators who have integrated big data into their practice have tried to raise awareness about and address concerns related to ethics, institutional capacity, and differences in values.

Despite the slow integration of big data into evaluation practice, a few examples from the international development community exist. For example, Lemire et al. (2020), when reflecting on the place of big data analytics on the method branch of the evaluation tree, identified applications of big data use in evaluation. These included predicting the career pathways of community college students using machine learning and predictive analytics (Cho, 2016), helping combat the Zika virus and malaria with smartphone and imagery technology (Abt Associates, 2017), and evaluating a 2012-2016 peacemaking process in Columbia with sentiment and social network analysis of tweets (Fabra-Mata & Mygind, 2019). These examples are notable in how they make use of big data (i.e., either data generation, data analytics, or data ecosystems). The Fabra-Mata and Mygind article is quite exemplary of the possibilities of harnessing big data ecosystems in evaluation efforts.

In addition to being original in its big data approach, the Fabra-Mata and Mygind article is of notable interest because, to the best of our knowledge, the article is the first published big data application in an evaluation-specific journal. The aims of Fabra-Mata and Mygind are remarkable as they provide insight into how big data and evaluation might occur in practice. Specifically, Fabra-Mata and Mygind used 4 years of Twitter data to (1) determine critical points in the peacemaking process in Columbia, (2) expand and deepen the evaluation's data collection reach (e.g., they included discourse from Colombian society at large instead of just direct participants in the peacemaking process), (3) identify relevant stakeholders in the peacemaking process (some who would possibly serve as key informants to interview as part of their broader evaluation efforts), and (4) gain insights that might enrich and contribute findings related to their specific evaluation questions. In reflecting on the usefulness of their social media analyses for their evaluation, Fabra-Mata and Mygind noted that their Twitter analysis helped strengthen multimethod triangulation and the validity of their evaluation findings.

Big data may find value and broader use in evaluation. However, for big data to be relevant and valuable to evaluation, the field will need more worked examples and discussion of big data methodology and evaluation. In this method note, we provide a case example of the implementation of LDA topic modeling (Blei & Lafferty, 2009), a big data or machine learning framework for text mining, and we attempt to highlight topic modeling's potential use in evaluation. In the next section, we begin this discussion by providing an overview of topic modeling.

Specifically, Fabra-Mata and Mygind used 4 years of Twitter data to (1) determine critical points in the peacemaking process in Columbia, (2) expand and deepen the evaluation's data collection reach (e.g., they included discourse from Colombian society at large instead of just direct participants in the peacemaking process), (3) identify relevant stakeholders in the peacemaking process (some who would possibly serve as key informants to interview as part of their broader evaluation efforts), and (4) gain insights that might enrich and contribute findings related to their specific evaluation questions. In reflecting on the usefulness of their social media analyses for their evaluation, Fabra-Mata and Mygind noted that their Twitter analysis helped strengthen multimethod triangulation and the validity of their evaluation findings.

Big data may find value and broader use in evaluation. However, for big data to be relevant and valuable to evaluation, the field will need more worked examples and discussion of big data methodology and evaluation. In this method note, we provide a case example of the implementation of LDA topic modeling (Blei & Lafferty, 2009), a big data or machine learning framework for text mining, and we attempt to highlight topic modeling's potential use in evaluation. In the next section, we begin this discussion by providing an overview of topic modeling.
sections.preview_head: For this method note, we define big data as "an 'integrated approach to research and development' (including development evaluation) that involves three interlinked components": data generation, data analytics, and data ecosystems (UN Global Pulse, 2016, p. 34). 3 Data generation involves the production and collection of large volumes of data. The deluge of big data produces data in various formats and content, regularly characterized as the variety of big data (e.g., see the five Vs of big data 4 in Katal et al., 2013). For example, big data is often characterized as coming in the form of either structured (e.g., a wide file with IDs, demographic variables, and variables of interest), semistructured (e.g., an XML log file of examinee test-taking behavior), or unstructured data (e.g., a file with a mix of images and text).

sections.paper_id: Cintron and Montrosse-Moorhead - 2022 - Integrating Big Data Into Evaluation R Code for Topic Identification and Modeling
paragraphs: As text information continues to be digitized and stored, it will become increasingly difficult to find and discover the textual information we are looking for (Blei, 2012). 5 This challenge has paved the way for new computational tools that help organize, search, and draw meaning from vast amounts of textual information. One such computational tool is topic modeling, the specific big data tool we focus on in this method note.

Topic modeling is a probabilistic machine learning approach for rapidly analyzing extensive collections of text data (Blei, 2012;Blei & Lafferty, 2009;Murphy, 2012). 6 Topic models can promptly reduce a collection of open-ended texts to a small number of themes (latent topics) that effectively represent the collection of open-ended texts (Arun et al., 2010). In short, topic modeling is an approach that can be utilized for the automatic classification of open-ended texts to uncover thematic structures in the set of open-ended texts (Arora et al., 2012). Essentially, topic modeling makes it possible for investigators to explore relationships with text data and make abductive inferences (Krippendorf, 2004) in a rapid time frame.

The open-ended texts or qualitative responses used in topic modeling, and in general text mining, are referred to as documents. Specifically, an individual document could be a sentence, a paragraph, or a complete novel. The documents are generally part of a collection (or text corpus in the topic modeling literature). That is, a text corpus is a collection of documents (e.g., a collection of sentences, paragraphs, or novels). For example, a collection of responses to an open-ended survey item would be referred to as a text corpus. In this way, each individual's response represents a document and the collection of all responses forms the text corpus. The choice of what represents a 415 Cintron and Montrosse-Moorhead document is chiefly an operational choice. In the case example of this method note, we use a tweet as the operational choice for the type of document, and the text corpus consists of a collection of tweets.

As noted above, topic modeling reduces a set of documents into a set of themes (latent topics). Specifically, each theme is characterized by a distribution over words, or more generally, N-grams. N-grams are a system of separating words in a document into a set or sequence of words. This process is known as "tokenizing" the data, and we might think of tokens as the set of N-grams. We could, for example, split a sentence into a sequence of individual words or tokens. This sequence of individual words would be referred to as a sequence of unigrams or 1-grams. When the words (tokens) in a document are unigrams, the document's words are a bag of words. Similarly, 2grams, also referred to as bigrams, are a set of two pair combinations of words. For example, the set of bigram tokens from the sentence "Jerry plays the banjo" are (1) Jerry plays, (2) plays the, and (3) the banjo. In general, the number of tokens in a set of words for a given document is given as Tokens ¼ X À (N À 1), where X is the number of words in a document and N is the N-gram choice.

In the example sentence about Jerry (X ¼ 4), the number of bigram tokens (N ¼ 2) in the sentence is Tokens ¼ 4 À (2 À 1) ¼ 3 whereas the number of unigram tokens (N ¼ 1) in the sentence is Tokens ¼ 4 À (1 À 1) ¼ 4. When using topic modeling, in the process of cleaning and preparing text data for analysis, we need to decide on the N-grams or tokens we wish to use. The topic model is then supplied with the set of N-grams or tokens that characterize the text corpus. The decision to use unigram tokens (thereby treating an individual document as a bag of words) is a common approach utilized in the literature on topic modeling (Hollibaugh, 2019;Roberts et al., 2016).

There are various methods or approaches for implementing topic modeling. These methods come with different assumptions. Three popular methods for topic modeling include latent semantic indexing (Papadimitriou et al., 2000), nonnegative matrix factorization (Pauca et al., 2004), and LDA. We do not discuss and contrast these methods here. 7 However, we note that our choice for focusing on LDA over the other topic modeling methods is because of the method's popularity in the machine learning community and its ability to handle multiple meanings of a word (i.e., polysemy). In the next section, we provide a brief introduction to LDA and then follow this up with a reflection on potential applications of LDA topic modeling in evaluation.

paragraphs: LDA was developed in the field of machine learning and computer science (Blei et al., 2003). LDA is a latent variable model for discrete data (Murphy, 2012). Here, the discrete data are words (other examples of discrete data are voting records or item responses). As a topic model, LDA is used to automate the discovery of themes (latent topics) in blocks of text or documents (Pritchard et al., 2000). With conjugate priors for the model parameters, the full LDA model is (Murphy, 2012, p. 954):

We do not go into any further technical detail about the LDA model or interpretation of the parameters here. However, a description of the parameters in the full model is in Table 1. For those readers interested, we suggest either Murphy (2012) or Blei et al. (2003). Instead, we provide an account of the model in simplified terms and ideas that describe how the aims of LDA topic modeling translate into the practice of LDA topic modeling in R. This account is in line with the practice of LDA topic modeling in R as reported in Silge and Robinson (2017).

To start, we note that in general, with topic modeling or mining, the goal is often to reduce a collection of documents into natural groups to understand the nature of the content they contain separately. LDA treats each document as a mixture of topics and each topic as a mixture of words. From this treatment, we can obtain document-topic probabilities and word-topic probabilities. In the examples below, we use the notation of Silge and Robinson (2017), where the document-topic probabilities are referred to as the g estimates (the document-topic probabilities correspond to Equation 2 in Murphy's full model), and the word-topic probabilities are referred to as the b estimates (the word-topic probabilities correspond to Equation 4 in Murphy's full model). 8 Silge and Robinson (2017) note, "This allows documents to 'overlap' each other in terms of content, rather than being separated into discrete groups, in a way that mirrors typical use of natural language" (section 6). Specifically, when we say that every document is a mixture of topics, we mean that each document can contain words on several topics in varying proportions. For example, we might say a research methodology book has 10% of its content covering the topic of "factor analysis," whereas 90% of its content covers the topic of "research methodology more generally." Furthermore, we might say a factor analysis book has 90% of its content covering the topic of "factor analysis," whereas 10% of its content covers the topic of "research methodology more generally."

LDA is a mathematical model for estimating the document-topic (g) and word-topic (b) probabilities simultaneously. That is, LDA finds a " . . . mixture of words that is associated with each topic, while also determining the mixture of topics that describes each document" (Silge & Robinson, 2017, section 6.1). In the case example below, we use R to simultaneously estimate and extract these two sets of probabilities. That is, we use R to extract: (1) word-topic probabilities (contained in the "beta" or b matrix in R) and (2) document-topic probabilities (contained in the "gamma" or g matrix in R). These matrices provide meaningful characterizations of text data and, in general, allow us to explore two substantive questions: Do the words of the document collectively define a set of themes? What topics define documents?

sections.paper_id: Cintron and Montrosse-Moorhead - 2022 - Integrating Big Data Into Evaluation R Code for Topic Identification and Modeling
paragraphs: Having provided a brief introduction to LDA, potential applications of LDA topic modeling in evaluation are discussed in this section. As a method for identifying themes (latent topics) from text data in documents, possible uses of topic modeling in evaluation may be related to using the 417 Cintron and Montrosse-Moorhead topics or themes that emerge from the text data about documents. For example, with topic modeling, we could uncover themes (latent topics) about documents, annotate the documents for these themes, and organize collections of documents based on these themes. The possible text data for use in topic modeling is wide-ranging and include, for example, social media (Schwartz et al., 2013), freeresponse data (Popping, 2015), news reports (Roberts et al., 2016), medical health records (Obeid et al., 2019), and public administration documents (Hollibaugh, 2019).

To the best of our knowledge, no existing published evaluation studies have used topic modeling, although there are useful research examples that illustrate how topic modeling may be used in evaluation. The first study is by Hollibaugh (2019). Hollibaugh, recognizing the massive amounts of text generated by government agencies and policy makers, used thousands of documents from the Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services to help explain changes in the agencies' priorities. As evaluation is an essential function of government, there may be other instances where value can be extracted from the massive amount of text generated by government agencies and policy makers. Such analyses may help provide critical information about the effectiveness and efficiency of various government programs or policies.

The second study is by Roberts et al. (2020). Roberts and colleagues were interested in the effects of social media censorship in China. The goal of Roberts and colleagues' study was to "estimate the causal effect of experiencing censorship on the subsequent censorship and posting rates of social media users in China" (p. 13). One problem in studying this issue is finding an appropriate comparison group for making causal claims, given that it would be unethical for researchers to censor people. Therefore, using the fact that the Chinese censorship regime made mistakes when attempting to censor social media users in China, Roberts and colleagues used topic modeling to "identify pairs of nearly identical social media posts written by nearly identical users, where one is censored and the other is not" (p. 13). With these matched groups, the causal effects of censorship on the subsequent censorship and posting rates of social media users were estimated. Through topic modeling, Roberts and colleagues provide a method for matching documents that allows one to match on the topical content of the documents. Methods for matching documents in evaluation would be useful for evaluators attempting to make causal claims regarding the differences between groups of documents (e.g., are there systematic differences between publicly available reports and those internal to a program).

Having discussed big data and evaluation, and topical modeling (specifically LDA) and its potential use in evaluation, in the next section, a case example of LDA topic modeling is presented. This case example describes a five-step model building process for LDA topic modeling using tweets that used the hashtag #equity. The #equity hashtag was intentionally selected given its importance in evaluation and the contexts in which evaluators work.

The #equity hashtag was intentionally selected given its importance in evaluation and the contexts in which evaluators work.
sections.text_with_footnotes:
Having provided a brief introduction to LDA, potential applications of LDA topic modeling in evaluation are discussed in this section. As a method for identifying themes (latent topics) from text data in documents, possible uses of topic modeling in evaluation may be related to using the 417 Cintron and Montrosse-Moorhead topics or themes that emerge from the text data about documents. For example, with topic modeling, we could uncover themes (latent topics) about documents, annotate the documents for these themes, and organize collections of documents based on these themes. The possible text data for use in topic modeling is wide-ranging and include, for example, social media (Schwartz et al., 2013), freeresponse data (Popping, 2015), news reports (Roberts et al., 2016), medical health records (Obeid et al., 2019), and public administration documents (Hollibaugh, 2019).

To the best of our knowledge, no existing published evaluation studies have used topic modeling, although there are useful research examples that illustrate how topic modeling may be used in evaluation. The first study is by Hollibaugh (2019). Hollibaugh, recognizing the massive amounts of text generated by government agencies and policy makers, used thousands of documents from the Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services to help explain changes in the agencies' priorities. As evaluation is an essential function of government, there may be other instances where value can be extracted from the massive amount of text generated by government agencies and policy makers. Such analyses may help provide critical information about the effectiveness and efficiency of various government programs or policies.

The second study is by Roberts et al. (2020). Roberts and colleagues were interested in the effects of social media censorship in China. The goal of Roberts and colleagues' study was to "estimate the causal effect of experiencing censorship on the subsequent censorship and posting rates of social media users in China" (p. 13). One problem in studying this issue is finding an appropriate comparison group for making causal claims, given that it would be unethical for researchers to censor people. Therefore, using the fact that the Chinese censorship regime made mistakes when attempting to censor social media users in China, Roberts and colleagues used topic modeling to "identify pairs of nearly identical social media posts written by nearly identical users, where one is censored and the other is not" (p. 13). With these matched groups, the causal effects of censorship on the subsequent censorship and posting rates of social media users were estimated. Through topic modeling, Roberts and colleagues provide a method for matching documents that allows one to match on the topical content of the documents. Methods for matching documents in evaluation would be useful for evaluators attempting to make causal claims regarding the differences between groups of documents (e.g., are there systematic differences between publicly available reports and those internal to a program).

sections.header_norm: what do we mean by equity a case example of lda topic modeling in r
paragraphs: This section outlines five critical model building steps for LDA topic modeling in R: (1) processing text data, (2) generating a document-term matrix (DTM), (3) deciding on the number of topics K, (4) implementing LDA topic modeling with K topics, and (5) interpreting the K topic solution. These steps are summarized in Figure 1 and build on topic modeling ideas provided in Silge and Robinson (2017).

The five steps to LDA topic modeling in R are demonstrated through our case example that includes 1,796 open-ended tweets from Twitter that use the hashtag #equity. The tweets for this study were extracted from the web using Twitter Archiver and only include tweets made on January 8, 2019. Twitter Archiver 9 is a software that has a free and premium version and provides services that enable the extraction of tweets from Twitter into a Google Spreadsheet for any search key word or hashtag. The details of using Twitter Archiver are discussed in the workbook. The five model building steps in this example and workbook generalize beyond the use of tweets as documents and may include any set of documents (i.e., corpus) a researcher wishes to operationalize. Also, there are other methods of extracting data from Twitter, including web scraping (Russell, 2011).

In our example, it is hypothesized that "equity" is a polysemic term and that this will bear out in LDA topic modeling results. We base this hypothesis on dictionary entries that suggest the term equity inherently has two meanings: (1) "justice according to natural law or right" (i.e., "freedom from bias or favoritism" or "something that is equitable") and ( 2) "the money value of property or of an interest in property in excess of claims or liens against it" or "the common stock of a corporation" (Merriam-Webster). From a quick visual scan of tweets in the data, this hypothesis appeared plausible. For example, we identified tweets that used the hashtag #equity in an economic sense, in a social justice sense, and in a combination of both senses. Consider the following tweet, that is, from our read, a tweet that uses equity in both the economic and social justice sense: 10 How do we boost businesses of color in North Carolina? We talked with policy analyst Will Munn with the @ncbudgetandtax about his recent report Powering Up Prosperity -#ncga #ncpol #ncgov #equity #entrepreneursofcolor #podcast. (@NCPolicyWatch)

paragraphs: Before diving into the steps for LDA topic modeling in R, we briefly describe the statistical software. R (R Core Team, 2017) is an open-source programming software for statistical computing and graphics. R enables users to perform statistical analysis, text mining, or natural language processing. R's core features, however, require some level of programming expertise and an understanding of libraries or packages that contain functions to perform specific tasks. For instance, one R package that may offer a free alternative to qualitative data analysis with NVivo (Richards, 1999) is RQDA (R-based Qualitative Data Analysis; Huang, 2012); see Chandra and Shang (2017) for an example of using RQDA for qualitative research. In the example below, several R packages are used to carry out the topic modeling analyses.

There are several resources to assist evaluators with learning and using R. For example, several open-source resources for learning R and guided handbooks are available (Field et al., 2012;Fox & Weisberg, 2011). Text Mining with R by Silge and Robinson (2017) has a companion website (https://www.tidytextmining.com/) that contains extensive code and guidance on text mining. Moreover, the R community is quite large and supportive (see, e.g., Smith, 2017), and many evaluators use R in their research (Tipton & Matlen, 2019;Westlund & Stuart, 2017).

Having provided a brief overview of the statistical software R, in what follows, the five model building steps for LDA topic modeling with R are described. We offer a big picture description of each step's value in the LDA topic modeling process and finer details on implementing each step. Moreover, to help evaluators understand the five steps and have a common example from which to anchor their learning, the steps are described using the hashtag #equity case example presented earlier.

sections.paper_id: Cintron and Montrosse-Moorhead - 2022 - Integrating Big Data Into Evaluation R Code for Topic Identification and Modeling
paragraphs: Step 1: Process Text Data

After importing a corpus of data into R (the collection of tweets in our example), Step 1 in the LDA topic modeling process involves processing or cleaning the text data in the corpus. This step is essential because text data are often messy and unstructured, and we need to make sure that we process our text data in a way that is defensible, reproducible, and aligned with the intended use. For example, in a text data corpus, there may be numbers and letters that are not actual words due to UTF-8 encoding (UTF-8 is a variable-width character encoding process typical of data on websites). These numbers and letters may be from "emojis" or "gifs." For example, the fingers crossed emoji " " has a UTF-8 encoding of "\U0001f91e." Therefore, the tweet text "Wishing you the best " in R would appear as "Wishing you the best \U0001f91e."

In the workbook for implementing LDA topic modeling that contains annotated code, we provide a basic function textprocess that manipulates the text in our corpus in a few ways to ensure the data are processed or cleaned reasonably well. The text manipulations provided by the textprocess function include (1) making the text lowercase, (2) removing punctuation, (3) removing alphanumeric characters, (4) removing symbols, and (5) removing special characters. After taking these steps, the data are cleaned of unwanted numbers, symbols, and characters and is in a suitable format for text mining. Note, this step may require additional or different data cleaning manipulations depending on the scope of one's analysis. The textprocess function aims to demonstrate text cleaning choices that are characteristic of typical text mining analyses. Other common procedures used in text mining and analyses that are not discussed here include, for example, lemmatization and stemming (see Silge & Robinson, 2017).

The next set of steps we use to process our data for LDA topic modeling include (1) tokenizing the data (here we create a bag of words or unigrams) and (2) removing stopwords. Tokenization is a way of operationalizing and extracting text features to be used in the topic modeling analysis. Stopwords are typically filtered out before analyzing text data and include commonly occurring words in a language (e.g., or, it, the). In R, to remove stopwords, we use the tidytext (Silge & Robinson, 2016) dictionary of stopwords. In the example analysis below, we decide to tokenize our data by splitting the data into sets of unigrams in each document. We also decide to remove stopwords.

An example may help illustrate what the process of tokenizing and removing stopwords entails and provide further clarity to what the unigrams are. Consider this oft-used quote by Stake: "When the cook tastes the soup, that's formative. When the guests taste the soup, that's summative." Here, we treat each sentence of the quote as a separate document, and thereby, our text corpus is a collection of two documents (sentences). Through tokenization, a master list of words is made by looking across all sentences within each of the documents (e.g., "when," "the," "cook," "tastes," "soup," "thats," "formative," "guests," "taste," "summative"). The frequency of words from the master list appearing in each document and across the text corpus can now be tabulated. In each document, all words would have a count of one except "the" that would have a count of two in each document. Likewise, in the text corpus, "the" has a count of four, "when" and "soup" a count of two, and the remaining words a count of one. In the LDA literature, each word is called a "gram." And, a unigram simply means that we counted words individually. In theory and in practice, it is possible to count two-word pairs (e.g., "when the," "the cook") or three-word clusters (e.g., "when the cook," "the cook tastes"), which are called bigrams and trigrams, respectively. We use unigrams in this example for ease of explanation and interpretation. In our example quote, had we removed "the" as a stopword, it would no longer appear as a word in the text corpus and therefore would no longer be the most frequently used word.

After processing the text data, it helps generate a figure of the N-gram frequencies, which visually shows the word counts summed across documents. Here, we report the N-gram (unigram) frequencies in Figure 2 (note words with frequencies less than 50 are not shown). We can see that the three most frequently mentioned words were diversity, market, and inclusion. Across all 1,796 documents (tweets corpus), these words were used over 150 times. Several words were used over 100 times. These words include education, nifty (related to India's national stock exchange), stockmarket, trading, investment, and students. The pattern of word frequencies suggests that on January 8, 2019, there was a preference for using the hashtag #equity on Twitter in an economic sense.

Step 2: Generate a DTM In Step 2, the LDA topic modeling process involves generating a DTM, which is used to evaluate how important each word is to a collection of documents (corpus). The DTM characterizes the importance of a particular word proportional to the number of times that word appears in a document and its frequency in the entire corpus. So, for example, words that frequently appear within a particular document and in the entire corpus are evaluated to be more important (i.e., given greater weight). This process is repeated for each word, and the results are put into a data matrix (i.e., the DTM). Generating the DTM is an essential step because the DTM is required for vital functions in both the topicmodels and ldatuning R packages.

More precisely, the DTM is a sparse matrix (i.e., the matrix contains many zeros) that describes a corpus where each row of the DTM is a document in the corpus, and each column of the DTM is a word (or token more generally). For LDA topic modeling, a cell of the DTM matrix typically includes a count of a term-frequency À inverse document frequency (tf À idf). The tf À idf is defined as tf À idf ¼ tf Â idf, where tf is the term (word or token) frequency in a document and idf is calculated as idf ¼ log(n d /(1 þ df)). Here, n d is the total number of documents, and df is the number of documents that contain a given term (word or token).

In R, we will utilize the dplyr (Wickham et al., 2015) and tidytext packages to create a DTM. The steps to create this DTM are (1) create a count of a specific word (token) for a given document (tweet) and then sort them by greatest frequency use, (2) create a count of the total number of words (tokens) used by a given document (tweet), and (3) join the two counts by document together into a matrix, and (4) cast the matrix into a DTM of our choice using tidytext (see workbook for details). From the matrix formed in Step 3, we can in Step 4, for example, use tidytext to cast the matrix into either a simple DTM with the document by word frequencies per cell or a DTM with the tf À idf in each cell. Further discussion of the DTM is left for the workbook.

Step 3: Decide on the Number of Topics for LDA In Step 3, the LDA topic modeling process involves deciding on the number of topics for our LDA topic model. The purpose of this step is to figure out how many themes or topics underlie the text data. The step is akin to exploratory factor analysis, where we attempt to demarcate the number of factors.

The package ldatuning (Nikita, 2016) provides functionality for finding the number of topics that underlie the data. Specifically, the ldatuning package provides access to four different methods to discover the number of topics: ( 1 2014) method, and (4) the Griffiths and Steyvers (2004) method. The FindTo-picsNumber function in the ldatuning package is used to find the number of topics. We provide a brief overview of the FindTopicsNumber function in Table 2. The key points to note are that we supply the FindTopicsNumber function with a DTM, the range of the number of topics to explore, the method(s) to determine the number of topics (i.e., one, all, or some combination of the four listed above), and an estimation method (i.e., "Gibbs" or "VEM"). Gibbs sampling is a Markov Monte Carlo Chain sampling technique, and VEM stands for variational expectation-maximization (see McGrory, 2012). For our analysis, we supply the function with the DTM generated in Step 2, we examine topic solutions for two to 16 topics in increments of two (i.e., eight different topic solutions), we use the Arun et al. (2010) and Deveaud et al. (2014) methods, and we use a VEM approach for estimation (see workbook for additional details). Figure 3 displays the results of the FindTo-picsNumber function.

The Arun et al. (2010) method to find the number of topics requires that we minimize the function, whereas the Deveaud et al. (2014) method requires that we maximize the function. From Figure 3, the two functions appear to be minimized and maximized at roughly 12 topics. However, for this article, we decided to proceed with the LDA solution for four topics. This decision was made because the four-topic solution yielded clearer interpretability than the 12-topic solution. Moreover, the four-topic solution provided a compact representation of an LDA solution that is useful for pedagogical purposes. Note, however, this choice was mostly arbitrary. In the workbook, two-, four-, eight-, 12-, and 16-topic solutions are provided for reference. In the Discussion section, we overview a few issues with the state-of-the-art methods for deciding on the number of topics for LDA. One thing to note here is that there does not appear to be a clear consensus regarding how to decide on the number of topics (see, e.g., discussion in Golino et al., 2020).
Step 4: Implement LDA With K Topics

In Step 4, the LDA topic modeling process involves estimating the LDA topic model with the number of topics that we decided on in Step 3 (here four topics). The package topicmodels (Hornik & Gru ¨n, 2011) allows us to implement (i.e., fit) the LDA model using the LDA function. The LDA function takes as inputs the DTM, the estimation method (here we use the VEM method), and a list of controls. An overview of the LDA function is provided in Table 3. Note that, in R, we are supplying the function with pieces of information (inputs or parameters) that are needed to run the LDA analysis. To extract and use the results from the LDA function, we also need to store these results as an object in R (see workbook for example R objects). The R object retains the information topics Vector with the number of topics to compare different models. In the Rmarkdown workbook we specify a vector that fits topic models with topics that range from 2 to 16 topics by two topics (i.e., eight models). metrics String or vector of possible metrics: "Griffiths2004," "CaoJuan2009," "Arun2010," and "Deveaud2014." In the Rmarkdown workbook, we only specify "Arun2010," "Deveaud2014." The citations for these papers can be found in the references of the paper.

Likewise, in the text corpus, "the" has a count of four, "when" and "soup" a count of two, and the remaining words a count of one. In the LDA literature, each word is called a "gram." And, a unigram simply means that we counted words individually. In theory and in practice, it is possible to count two-word pairs (e.g., "when the," "the cook") or three-word clusters (e.g., "when the cook," "the cook tastes"), which are called bigrams and trigrams, respectively. We use unigrams in this example for ease of explanation and interpretation. In our example quote, had we removed "the" as a stopword, it would no longer appear as a word in the text corpus and therefore would no longer be the most frequently used word.

After processing the text data, it helps generate a figure of the N-gram frequencies, which visually shows the word counts summed across documents. Here, we report the N-gram (unigram) frequencies in Figure 2 (note words with frequencies less than 50 are not shown). We can see that the three most frequently mentioned words were diversity, market, and inclusion. Across all 1,796 documents (tweets corpus), these words were used over 150 times. Several words were used over 100 times. These words include education, nifty (related to India's national stock exchange), stockmarket, trading, investment, and students. The pattern of word frequencies suggests that on January 8, 2019, there was a preference for using the hashtag #equity on Twitter in an economic sense.

Step 2: Generate a DTM In Step 2, the LDA topic modeling process involves generating a DTM, which is used to evaluate how important each word is to a collection of documents (corpus). The DTM characterizes the importance of a particular word proportional to the number of times that word appears in a document and its frequency in the entire corpus. So, for example, words that frequently appear within a particular document and in the entire corpus are evaluated to be more important (i.e., given greater weight). This process is repeated for each word, and the results are put into a data matrix (i.e., the DTM). Generating the DTM is an essential step because the DTM is required for vital functions in both the topicmodels and ldatuning R packages.

More precisely, the DTM is a sparse matrix (i.e., the matrix contains many zeros) that describes a corpus where each row of the DTM is a document in the corpus, and each column of the DTM is a word (or token more generally). For LDA topic modeling, a cell of the DTM matrix typically includes a count of a term-frequency À inverse document frequency (tf À idf). The tf À idf is defined as tf À idf ¼ tf Â idf, where tf is the term (word or token) frequency in a document and idf is calculated as idf ¼ log(n d /(1 þ df)). Here, n d is the total number of documents, and df is the number of documents that contain a given term (word or token).

In R, we will utilize the dplyr (Wickham et al., 2015) and tidytext packages to create a DTM. The steps to create this DTM are (1) create a count of a specific word (token) for a given document (tweet) and then sort them by greatest frequency use, (2) create a count of the total number of words (tokens) used by a given document (tweet), and (3) join the two counts by document together into a matrix, and (4) cast the matrix into a DTM of our choice using tidytext (see workbook for details). From the matrix formed in Step 3, we can in Step 4, for example, use tidytext to cast the matrix into either a simple DTM with the document by word frequencies per cell or a DTM with the tf À idf in each cell. Further discussion of the DTM is left for the workbook.

Step 3: Decide on the Number of Topics for LDA In Step 3, the LDA topic modeling process involves deciding on the number of topics for our LDA topic model. The purpose of this step is to figure out how many themes or topics underlie the text data. The step is akin to exploratory factor analysis, where we attempt to demarcate the number of factors.

The package ldatuning (Nikita, 2016) provides functionality for finding the number of topics that underlie the data. Specifically, the ldatuning package provides access to four different methods to discover the number of topics: ( 1 2014) method, and (4) the Griffiths and Steyvers (2004) method. The FindTo-picsNumber function in the ldatuning package is used to find the number of topics. We provide a brief overview of the FindTopicsNumber function in Table 2. The key points to note are that we supply the FindTopicsNumber function with a DTM, the range of the number of topics to explore, the method(s) to determine the number of topics (i.e., one, all, or some combination of the four listed above), and an estimation method (i.e., "Gibbs" or "VEM"). Gibbs sampling is a Markov Monte Carlo Chain sampling technique, and VEM stands for variational expectation-maximization (see McGrory, 2012). For our analysis, we supply the function with the DTM generated in Step 2, we examine topic solutions for two to 16 topics in increments of two (i.e., eight different topic solutions), we use the Arun et al. (2010) and Deveaud et al. (2014) methods, and we use a VEM approach for estimation (see workbook for additional details). Figure 3 displays the results of the FindTo-picsNumber function.

The Arun et al. (2010) method to find the number of topics requires that we minimize the function, whereas the Deveaud et al. (2014) method requires that we maximize the function. From Figure 3, the two functions appear to be minimized and maximized at roughly 12 topics. However, for this article, we decided to proceed with the LDA solution for four topics. This decision was made because the four-topic solution yielded clearer interpretability than the 12-topic solution. Moreover, the four-topic solution provided a compact representation of an LDA solution that is useful for pedagogical purposes. Note, however, this choice was mostly arbitrary. In the workbook, two-, four-, eight-, 12-, and 16-topic solutions are provided for reference. In the Discussion section, we overview a few issues with the state-of-the-art methods for deciding on the number of topics for LDA. One thing to note here is that there does not appear to be a clear consensus regarding how to decide on the number of topics (see, e.g., discussion in Golino et al., 2020).

Step 4: Implement LDA With K Topics

In Step 4, the LDA topic modeling process involves estimating the LDA topic model with the number of topics that we decided on in Step 3 (here four topics). The package topicmodels (Hornik & Gru ¨n, 2011) allows us to implement (i.e., fit) the LDA model using the LDA function. The LDA function takes as inputs the DTM, the estimation method (here we use the VEM method), and a list of controls. An overview of the LDA function is provided in Table 3. Note that, in R, we are supplying the function with pieces of information (inputs or parameters) that are needed to run the LDA analysis. To extract and use the results from the LDA function, we also need to store these results as an object in R (see workbook for example R objects). The R object retains the information topics Vector with the number of topics to compare different models. In the Rmarkdown workbook we specify a vector that fits topic models with topics that range from 2 to 16 topics by two topics (i.e., eight models). metrics String or vector of possible metrics: "Griffiths2004," "CaoJuan2009," "Arun2010," and "Deveaud2014." In the Rmarkdown workbook, we only specify "Arun2010," "Deveaud2014." The citations for these papers can be found in the references of the paper.

For example, in a text data corpus, there may be numbers and letters that are not actual words due to UTF-8 encoding (UTF-8 is a variable-width character encoding process typical of data on websites). These numbers and letters may be from "emojis" or "gifs." For example, the fingers crossed emoji " " has a UTF-8 encoding of "\U0001f91e." Step 5: Interpret K Topic Solution

The last step in the LDA topic modeling process is to interpret the LDA topic modeling solution. The purpose of this step is to generate an inference from the LDA topic model results. The step is akin to exploratory factor analysis where we attempt to name or label the factors.

The first figure we use to interpret our LDA topic solution is Figure 4. In Figure 4, we can see the top 15 terms for the four topics in our LDA solution. The first topic's five most probable words were investing, market, finance, business, and stockmarket. Upon full review of the distribution of words in Topic 1, we also see terms such as funding, wealth, debt, and investment. Therefore, we interpret this topic as equity in an investment or capital sense (i.e., building wealth or capital). In this interpretation, the weight is given to words that are related to equity in terms of wealth creation (e.g., investing, market, finance).

The second topic's five most probable words were diversity, inclusion, amp, 11 equality, and justice. Upon full review of the distribution of words for Topic 2, we see additional terms such as people, women, students, and leadership. Therefore, we interpret this topic as equity being used to discuss social justice or fairness. In this interpretation, the weight is given to words such as equity and justice and the different types of groups referenced (e.g., women and students). In terms of equity being used to discuss social justice or fairness, it is typical that groups are identified and contrasted because inequality implies a difference in some outcome between some groups of interest (e.g., inequality in opportunities by gender).

The third topic's five most probable words were diversity, inclusion, students, education, and people. Upon full review of the distribution of words for the third topic, we believe that this topic is about equity but focused on education (e.g., highered, college, school, and teachers, emerged as essential words). Therefore, we would refer to the third topic as equity being used to discuss equity in higher education. In this interpretation, the weight is given to words such as highered, college, school, and teachers.

Lastly, the fourth topic's five most probable words were nifty, stock market, stocks, market, and stock. Upon full review of the distribution of words for the fourth topic, we see that the topic includes words for different stock exchanges (e.g., SENSEX, Bombay Stock Exchange, National Stock Exchange of India) and words such as trade, investors, and commodity. Therefore, we would refer to the fourth topic as economic equity focused on the stock market.

It is important to reflect here on the topics and interpretations of the topics. Human judgment is required here to make sense of the topics and discover the practical utility of the topics that are identified by the LDA analysis. There are some evaluations of topic quality that we can inspect (discussed next); however, it is the reflections of the researcher, evaluator, and/or stakeholders that drive the sensibility and utility of the topics.

Figures 5 and 6 help provide a sense of the quality of our topics. Therefore, we interpret this topic as equity in an investment or capital sense (i.e., building wealth or capital). In this interpretation, the weight is given to words that are related to equity in terms of wealth creation (e.g., investing, market, finance).

The second topic's five most probable words were diversity, inclusion, amp, 11 equality, and justice. Upon full review of the distribution of words for Topic 2, we see additional terms such as people, women, students, and leadership. Therefore, we interpret this topic as equity being used to discuss social justice or fairness. In this interpretation, the weight is given to words such as equity and justice and the different types of groups referenced (e.g., women and students). In terms of equity being used to discuss social justice or fairness, it is typical that groups are identified and contrasted because inequality implies a difference in some outcome between some groups of interest (e.g., inequality in opportunities by gender).

The third topic's five most probable words were diversity, inclusion, students, education, and people. Upon full review of the distribution of words for the third topic, we believe that this topic is about equity but focused on education (e.g., highered, college, school, and teachers, emerged as essential words). Therefore, we would refer to the third topic as equity being used to discuss equity in higher education. In this interpretation, the weight is given to words such as highered, college, school, and teachers.

Lastly, the fourth topic's five most probable words were nifty, stock market, stocks, market, and stock. Upon full review of the distribution of words for the fourth topic, we see that the topic includes words for different stock exchanges (e.g., SENSEX, Bombay Stock Exchange, National Stock Exchange of India) and words such as trade, investors, and commodity. Therefore, we would refer to the fourth topic as economic equity focused on the stock market.

It is important to reflect here on the topics and interpretations of the topics. Human judgment is required here to make sense of the topics and discover the practical utility of the topics that are identified by the LDA analysis. There are some evaluations of topic quality that we can inspect (discussed next); however, it is the reflections of the researcher, evaluator, and/or stakeholders that drive the sensibility and utility of the topics.

Figures 5 and 6 help provide a sense of the quality of our topics. The distribution of the probabilities for all topics across all tweets in the corpus is in Figure 5, whereas Figure 6 provides the distribution of the probabilities by topic. The seed control is used to set a seed to avoid changing the seed of the random number generator used to fit the topic model. estimate.alpha ¼ T by default so that the value of a is estimated from the data. estimate.beta ¼ T so that the value of a is estimated from the data. model Object of class "LDA" for initialization.

As with any new method that is being considered for adoption in a new context or setting, critical thinking must be used to reason through the adaption process. With this in mind and to help guide evaluators using LDA topic modeling in practice, Table 4 provides guidance on performing each LDA step within an evaluation context.

paragraphs: American Journal of Evaluation 43(3) positive side, there are several advantages to using LDA in evaluation. Notably, the free, opensource packages in R for topic modeling help make the implementation of LDA inexpensive. Moreover, as a computer-assisted method, LDA topic modeling may be implemented in a systematic manner within a reasonable time frame. The major challenges and costs associated with implementing an LDA topic model are likely to be in the form of either technical expertise or the collection of text data. The cost of technical expertise cannot be avoided and is important to ensuring that LDA topic models are used appropriately. This method note hopefully helps reduce some of the costs of technical expertise by introducing topic modeling and providing guidance on using LDA topic modeling in R. The cost of collecting text data may also be unavoidable in many scenarios. However, and fortunately, there are many solutions to collecting text that can be inexpensive (e.g., Twitter Archiver). Furthermore, evaluation programs or agencies may have many forms of text data that are already readily available for analysis with LDA topic modeling (e.g., interview data or prior evaluation reports).

sections.header_raw: Topic Modeling Versus Existing Approaches to the Quantitative Analysis of Text Data
sections.header_norm: topic modeling versus existing approaches to the quantitative analysis of text data
paragraphs: The collection and use of different types of text data in evaluation are not uncommon (Thomas, 2006). Two more traditional approaches for text analysis include, for example, content analysis and thematic analysis (Neuendorf, 2017(Neuendorf, , 2019)). The goal of thematic analysis is to qualitatively "develop a story from the texts of interest" (Neuendorf, 2019, p. 213). The goal of content analysis, on the other hand, is quantitative and includes "the systematic, objective, quantitative analysis of message characteristics" (Neuendorf, 2017, p. 1). Our view is that topic modeling is akin to thematic analysis and has the potential to overcome some of its shortcomings. Thematic analysis, for example, is limited in its cost-effectiveness and the number of individuals for whom data could be gathered and efficiently analyzed, especially in a short and reasonable time frame (Patton, 2015). Because time and money are not unlimited, evaluators may find themselves in Figure 6. The distribution of document by topic probabilities for each of the four topics. Note. The numbers represent Topics 1-4, respectively. Note that the y-axis is transformed to a log10 scale. For each topic, this figure demonstrates that there are many values near 0 and 1. This finding indicates that there are many documents that do and do not belong in each topic. The distributions here indicate that the documents are being differentiated well as belonging to a given topic or not (i.e., the probabilities of belonging to any given topic for each document are either near 0 or 1).

a situation where they are constrained on what they can do through thematic analysis. An evaluator seeking to review publications produced by their clients as part of a systematic review or as part of describing a program could encounter a scenario where hundreds of reports (interim and final), study briefs, program documentation, and so on have been produced. Depending on the time allotted for this activity, the budget, or both, the evaluator may be limited in what can be gathered from a thematic analysis. Likewise, an evaluator working on a multisite evaluation, in which they are collecting both quantitative and qualitative data from various stakeholders via surveys, could find themselves in the situation of having thousands of responses to one or more open-ended questions.

Here too, what an evaluator can do through traditional thematic analysis methods might be constrained. In short, the cost and labor intensity of the method and the reliance on human coders for forming a dictionary or algorithm for the analysis are legitimate and noted concerns (Burscher, 2016;Krippendorff, 2004).
Topic modeling offers a viable option for synthesizing text data, especially when there is a desire to analyze an extensive collection of text data directly and cost-effectively. The topic modeling

sections.paper_id: Cintron and Montrosse-Moorhead - 2022 - Integrating Big Data Into Evaluation R Code for Topic Identification and Modeling
paragraphs: Step 0: Gather text data Many forms of text data exist (e.g., social media, free-response data, news reports, medical health records). When gathering text data, it is crucial to have a sense of what the document level is for the corpus you plan to create. In this study, the document level was an individual tweet, and our corpus was a collection of tweets. A corpus, however, can be operationalized as a researcher or evaluator sees fit. For example, the text within individual evaluation reports could be the document level and the corpus a collection of evaluation reports.

Step 1: Process text data Processing or cleaning text data is an essential step in any method for analyzing textual data. The inclusion or exclusion of words has the potential to alter an analysis fundamentally by changing the representation of words in a corpus. Furthermore, processes such as lemmatization and stemming should be done with care as these decisions can also alter the meaning of the text data.

Step 2: Generate DTM Generating a DTM is a routine process that is necessary to perform LDA. The DTM is critical because it provides a sense of the joint distribution of documents and words in a corpus. The DTM is also useful for other text mining and topic modeling methods, including latent semantic analysis and singular value decomposition.

Step 3: Choose K topics Unfortunately, there is some concern about the appropriate methodology for choosing K topics, thereby increasing the importance of human judgment in choosing the number of topics.

For choosing the number of topics, we suggest using a process of triangulation among multiple methodologies as well as topic interpretability and discrimination (see discussion of Figures 5 and 6). Steps 4 and 5: Implement and interpret LDA with K topics Human judgment is required here to make sense of the topics as well as discover the practical utility of the topics that are identified by the LDA analysis-as such, choosing the number of topics is critical to topics sensibility and utility. If the number of topics to choose is not clear from Step 3, we recommend cycling back and forth between Steps 3 and 4, if necessary, to improve the sensibility and utility of the topics from LDA.
Note. LDA ¼ latent Dirichlet allocation; DTM ¼ Document-Term Matrix.

429 Cintron and Montrosse-Moorhead method overcomes the limitations of thematic analysis because topic modeling directly analyzes stakeholder interviews as a complete collection and enables rapid abductive inferences in a timely, cost-efficient manner. Furthermore, although there is some subjectivity when labeling topics, there are also diagnostics for the LDA method (e.g., the distribution of the probabilities for all topics and disaggregated by topic) that are not present in traditional thematic analysis methods. And not unlike the main aims of thematic analysis as a quantitative method, topic modeling has the advantage of machine automation and computation for the derivation of inferences from text data, which allow for the assessment of reliability and replicability (Dicle & Dicle, 2018).

The DTM is also useful for other text mining and topic modeling methods, including latent semantic analysis and singular value decomposition.

Step 3: Choose K topics Unfortunately, there is some concern about the appropriate methodology for choosing K topics, thereby increasing the importance of human judgment in choosing the number of topics.

For choosing the number of topics, we suggest using a process of triangulation among multiple methodologies as well as topic interpretability and discrimination (see discussion of Figures 5 and 6). Steps 4 and 5: Implement and interpret LDA with K topics Human judgment is required here to make sense of the topics as well as discover the practical utility of the topics that are identified by the LDA analysis-as such, choosing the number of topics is critical to topics sensibility and utility. If the number of topics to choose is not clear from Step 3, we recommend cycling back and forth between Steps 3 and 4, if necessary, to improve the sensibility and utility of the topics from LDA.

Note. LDA ¼ latent Dirichlet allocation; DTM ¼ Document-Term Matrix.

429 Cintron and Montrosse-Moorhead method overcomes the limitations of thematic analysis because topic modeling directly analyzes stakeholder interviews as a complete collection and enables rapid abductive inferences in a timely, cost-efficient manner. Furthermore, although there is some subjectivity when labeling topics, there are also diagnostics for the LDA method (e.g., the distribution of the probabilities for all topics and disaggregated by topic) that are not present in traditional thematic analysis methods. And not unlike the main aims of thematic analysis as a quantitative method, topic modeling has the advantage of machine automation and computation for the derivation of inferences from text data, which allow for the assessment of reliability and replicability (Dicle & Dicle, 2018).

sections.paper_id: Cintron and Montrosse-Moorhead - 2022 - Integrating Big Data Into Evaluation R Code for Topic Identification and Modeling
sections.header_raw: Potential Applications of Topic Modeling in Evaluation
sections.header_norm: potential applications of topic modeling in evaluation
paragraphs: Throughout this article, we have highlighted potential uses of topic modeling in evaluation. Many of these were examples of straightforward, direct analysis of text data in evaluation. There are, however, other possible applications of topic modeling in evaluation, which are described in this section. Collectively, our examples are illustrative, not exhaustive, and we hope readers will identify other potential applications of topic modeling in evaluation.

A potentially exciting example of where topic modeling may be of use in evaluation is in the process of gathering stakeholder feedback to focus an evaluation design. That is, how can we gather text data from stakeholders rapidly and make sense of these data in a timely, cost-efficient manner? The idea of focusing an evaluation design is embedded in several evaluation theories, approaches, models, and frameworks. For example, the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC) framework for program evaluation (Milstein et al., 2000) has this as one of the steps that evaluators do. In focusing an evaluation design within the CDC framework for program evaluation, after a consensus is reached about the program, an evaluation must consider the questions that are most relevant or important to an evaluation and the most appropriate evaluation design to answer these questions. Thematic annotations or topics of the text data provided by stakeholders may offer an opportunity to determine what themes, ideas, or questions are most relevant to an evaluation. Stakeholders at every level (e.g., recipients, implementers, designers, funders, communities served, or impacted) could be asked to respond to the open-ended item "What are the most important factors that we should consider in this evaluation?" The themes (latent topics) that are derived from LDA topic modeling could provide a framework for directing the aims or questions of an evaluation that best reflect the respondent's text data and that come from a more extensive set of stakeholders.

LDA topic modeling also provides an opportunity for evaluators to think creatively about how to elicit text data from survey respondents. For example, Salganik (2018) discusses the development of survey sampling modes in terms of eras. The first survey mode era was face-to-face (first era), the second survey mode era was telephone (second era), and the third (current) survey mode era is computers and mobile phones. Salganik (2018) notes how the third survey mode era offers greater flexibility in the "how" we ask questions. For example, Salganik cites Schober and Conrad (2015), who sent micro-surveys as a method to collect survey responses. Schober and Conrad (2015) found that these micro-surveys led to higher data quality than traditional voice interviews. The use of topic modeling provides an impetus for evaluators to use new methods of eliciting responses and to collect nontraditional text data that benefit from computer or mobile device survey administration. Such nontraditional data collection methods may aid in gathering potentially more robust and generalizable text data. Further, such use of third-era survey data can help address social desirability bias or interviewer effects (Salganik, 2018).

Another area where LDA topic modeling may prove useful is for predictive modeling of text data. LDA topic modeling can be used to build a predictive model of raw text data to classifying incoming documents or responses. Potential applications of this type of analysis would allow evaluators to identify text data use that deviates from some expected or predicted use of the text data (i.e., identify outliers based on where they were predicted to be). This use of topic modeling may be useful when eliciting feedback from raters used in an evaluation, for example, raters who are observing program or intervention implementation and completing an implementation quality, implementation fidelity, or implementation adaptation form across multiple sites and/or time periods. Such an evaluation of raters may aid in both the identification of rater effects and claims about program quality, implementation, and adaptation.

Lastly, several extensions to LDA exist, including the author-topic model (Rosen-Zvi et al., 2004), the geographic-topic model (Eisenstein et al., 2010), the dynamic-topic model (Blei & Lafferty, 2006), and the structured topic model (Roberts et al., 2016). These additional topic models offer several ways of extending LDA to allow the number of topics and words associated with a topic to vary by author, by geographic region, overtime or longitudinally, and by covariates, respectively. These extensions may offer unique opportunities for conducting research on evaluation (RoE) as well. For example, the author-topic model, the geographic-topic model, and the dynamic-topic model could be used to conduct a systematic review of evaluation research by author, geographic region, or longitudinally.
