sections.paper_id: Cintron and Montrosse-Moorhead - 2022 - Integrating Big Data Into Evaluation R Code for Topic Identification and Modeling
sections.header_raw: Shortcoming and Limitations of LDA Topic Modeling
sections.header_norm: shortcoming and limitations of lda topic modeling
paragraphs: While LDA topic modeling has many advantages and potential uses in evaluation, like any methodological tool, it is not without its shortcomings and limitations. We review two of the most important ones for evaluators to consider in practice.

The first shortcoming of LDA topic modeling is that the process of interpreting the themes (latent topics) is subjective. LDA topic modeling is not alone in this shortcoming. This shortcoming is present in any qualitative analysis. As Ratner (2002) notes, Qualitative methodology recognizes that the subjectivity of the researcher is intimately involved in scientific research. Subjectivity guides everything from the choice of topic that one studies, to formulating hypotheses, to selecting methodologies, and interpreting data. (p. 1) Furthermore, this shortcoming is not relegated to only qualitative analyses. For example, the subjectivity of factor analysis has long been recognized (Horn, 1967). Specifically, in exploratory factor analysis, " . . . the researcher is forced to make an ambiguous subjective judgment about the number of factors to retain" (Reio & Shuck, 2015, p. 18).

Another shortcoming of LDA topic modeling is that it is unclear what the appropriate number of documents or length of documents is. From a review of the literature, the authors were only able to identify one paper regarding this subject. Tang et al. (2014) conducted a simulation study to explore the impact of document length (N), the number of documents (D), and LDA hyperparameters on the recovery of topics in LDA topic modeling. While specific numeric guidelines are not provided, Tang et al. (2014) do provide the following general guidelines,

(1) The number of documents plays perhaps the most important role; it is theoretically impossible to guarantee identification of topics from a small number of documents, no matter how long. Once there are sufficiently many documents, further increasing the number may not significantly improve the performance unless the document length is also suitably increased . . . . (2) The length of documents also plays a crucial role: poor performance of the LDA is expected when documents are too short, even if there is a very large number of them. Ideally, the documents need to be sufficiently long, but not too long . . . . (3) When a very large number of topics than needed are used to fit the LDA, the statistical inference may become inescapably inefficient . . . . (4) The LDA performs well when the underlying topics are wellseparated in the sense of Euclidean metric; e.g. this is the case if these topics are concentrated at a small number of words. Another favorable scenario is concerned with the distribution of documents within the topic polytope: 12 when individual documents are associated mostly with small subsets of topics, so that they are geometrically concentrated most near the boundary of the topic polytope . . . . (5) If it is believed that each document is associated with few topics, the Dirichlet parameter of the document topic distributions should be set small (e.g., a % 0.01). If the topics are known to be word-sparse, the Dirichlet parameter of the word distributions b is set small (e.g., 0.01), in which case learning is efficient. Large b means more word-diffuse and similar topics, which might be inefficient to learn. (p. 197) Therefore, the misspecification of the LDA model could be present in the current analysis. For instance, the choice of four topics was simply chosen for interpretability and didactic reasons. Furthermore, there is no clear consensus on the best method for determining the number of topics/themes (Golino et al., 2020). In this study, no extensive sensitivity analyses were undertaken to explore this issue. Nonetheless, the solution that was derived for this topic modeling analysis involved thousands of documents and resulted in an interpretable solution that may be useful to characterize the language or content use of tweets that include the hashtag #equity.

In taking on an LDA topic modeling project, evaluators must consider critical assumptions about the cleaning of the text, the number of topics, and the quality of their data. Without proper care and supervision, topic models may produce "biased, inconsistent, or even meaningless information" (Vaessen et al., 2020, p. 137). Lastly, the representation of the documents in a corpus can impact the internal and external validity of the LDA topics. A representative sample of documents (e.g., a subset of books on factor analysis) should be internally valid (i.e., representative of the subject of factor analysis; comprehensible) and include documents that provide strong external validity to the target population of documents (e.g., all books on factor analysis). The importance of document representation is not unlike the importance of a representative sample in standard statistical analyses. In the case example herein, only 1 day of tweets is used and is unlikely to provide strong external validity claims.

In the case example herein, only 1 day of tweets is used and is unlikely to provide strong external validity claims.

sections.header_raw: Final Thoughts on Topic Modeling for Evaluators
sections.header_norm: final thoughts on topic modeling for evaluators
paragraphs: Topic modeling offers a potentially new and exciting avenue for evaluators, especially those using text data in practice or in their research. Topic modeling can offer new ways of conducting research and evaluation and RoE with text data. Topic modeling may offer evaluators a systematic, costeffective way of analyzing troves of unrealized text data that already exist about their program. Furthermore, topic modeling with text data may provide ideas and stimulate thought about why and how a program works to achieve some outcomes or goals. For example, topic modeling may help evaluators understand simple process evaluation questions such as "What were the barriers of implementation?" for a program by asking stakeholders their thoughts. Or, topic modeling may allow evaluators to conduct an outcome evaluation by evaluating whether there were substantial knowledge differences between groups of participants in a program versus those not in a program based on open-ended interviews. Hopefully, topic modeling may be of considerable value to the field of evaluation. Indeed, its value will ultimately be determined by the extent to which it is used in evaluation practice. To that end, our aim is that this article and the associated workbook will not only inspire evaluators to use topic modeling when and where appropriate but also make the transition from want-to-use to know-how-to-use easier.
